content.text:
Good — let me categorize all 64 columns cleanly. The sheet is really **two tables fused per row**: the minute-level signal (18 columns) and the contract metadata (46 columns, repeated identically for every minute of the same contract).

## Group A — Per-minute signal (18 columns — *this is the data*)

| # | Column | Type | What it is | Useful? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `Unnamed: 0` | int | Spreadsheet row index, artifact of export | Drop |
| 2 | `ticker` | str | Team-side contract id (`KXNBAGAME-<date><away><home>-<team>`) | **Key** — one series per ticker |
| 3 | `end_period_ts` | str (parse-flaky) | End-of-minute timestamp | Use `dt` instead |
| 4 | `dt` | str (ISO8601) | Datetime of the minute bucket (UTC) | **Key** — time axis |
| 5 | `price_open` | float | First **trade** price in the minute | For angle A `P(t)` |
| 6 | `price_high` | float | Max trade price in the minute | Range / volatility |
| 7 | `price_low` | float | Min trade price in the minute | Range / volatility |
| 8 | `price_close` | float | Last trade price in the minute | Canonical `P(t)` |
| 9 | `price_mean` | float | Mean trade price over the minute | Smoothed `P(t)` — good angle-A candidate |
| 10 | `price_previous` | float | Trade price at end of the *prior* minute | Redundant — equals `price_close` shifted |
| 11 | `yes_bid_open` | float | First bid quote in the minute | Quote-based features |
| 12 | `yes_bid_high` | float | Max bid in the minute | Quote range |
| 13 | `yes_bid_low` | float | Min bid in the minute | Quote range |
| 14 | `yes_bid_close` | float | Last bid at minute end | **Best for mid price** |
| 15 | `yes_ask_open` | float | First ask quote in the minute | Quote-based features |
| 16 | `yes_ask_high` | float | Max ask in the minute | Quote range |
| 17 | `yes_ask_low` | float | Min ask in the minute | Quote range |
| 18 | `yes_ask_close` | float | Last ask at minute end | **Best for mid price** |
| 19 | `volume` | float | Trades executed in this minute (contract count) | Activity proxy |
| 20 | `open_interest` | float | OI at end of the minute | Stock-vs-flow measure |

**Derived columns you'll want to build (not in the file):**
- `mid_close = (yes_bid_close + yes_ask_close) / 2` — quote-based mid, immune to bid-ask bounce
- `spread = yes_ask_close − yes_bid_close` — spread dynamics → angle C / stylized-facts
- `log_return = log(price_close / price_previous)` — for volatility clustering analysis
- `range_minute = price_high − price_low` — intra-minute realised vol proxy

## Group B — Contract metadata (46 columns, identical across every minute of the same contract)

These repeat verbatim for each row of a given contract. For analysis you only need them once, via `df.drop_duplicates('ticker')`.

**Identifiers (5):**
- `ticker` — team-side contract (same as Group A #2)
- `event_ticker` — game id (= ticker without the team suffix)
- `yes_sub_title` — YES-side team name
- `no_sub_title` — NO-side team name
- `title` — human title, e.g. "Indiana at Washington Winner?"

**Settlement + result (7) — angle-A ground truth:**
- `result` — `"yes"` or `"no"` (the side that won)
- `settlement_value_dollars` — 0 or 1 (or the rare fractional value for contested games)
- `settlement_ts` — when Kalshi settled the market
- `settlement_timer_seconds` — always 30
- `status` — always `"finalized"` in this file
- `expiration_value` — plain-text outcome description (often NaN before resolution)
- `last_price_dollars` — end-of-life trade price (redundant with the last `price_close` in Group A)

**Timestamps (7) — some you'll use, most you won't:**
- `open_time` — market open (useful for filtering pre-game activity)
- `close_time` — market close (useful for filtering post-game pinning)
- `created_time` — row creation in Kalshi system
- `updated_time` — last metadata touch
- `expected_expiration_time`, `expiration_time`, `latest_expiration_time` — planned/actual expiry, largely redundant for settled games

**End-of-life snapshots (8) — redundant with Group A or with settlement:**
- `previous_price_dollars`, `previous_yes_bid_dollars`, `previous_yes_ask_dollars` — snapshots
- `yes_bid_dollars`, `yes_ask_dollars`, `yes_bid_size_fp`, `yes_ask_size_fp` — close-state quote + size
- `no_bid_dollars`, `no_ask_dollars` — NO side at close
- `liquidity_dollars`, `open_interest_fp`, `volume_24h_fp`, `volume_fp` — aggregate stats at snapshot

**Market structure + rules (12) — metadata, basically constants per contract:**
- `market_type` — always `"binary"` here
- `tick_size` — `1` (cent grid: price lives on 0.01 steps)
- `strike_type` — `"structured"`
- `custom_strike` / `custom_strike_basketball_team` — team UUID
- `price_level_structure` — `"linear_cent"` (confirms the 0.01 grid)
- `price_ranges` — allowed price range (`{start: 0, end: 1, step: 0.01}`)
- `response_price_units` — `"usd_cent"`
- `notional_value_dollars` — contract notional = $1
- `fractional_trading_enabled` — bool
- `can_close_early` — bool
- `early_close_condition` — rule text
- `rules_primary`, `rules_secondary` — full resolution rules

**Identifiers you'll never use (3):**
- `Unnamed: 0` — export artifact

## The punchy summary

From 64 columns, only **~10 are load-bearing** for your project:

```
Time:   dt
Key:    ticker  (unique per contract; use event_ticker to cross-join with Polymarket)
Price:  price_close, price_mean
Quote:  yes_bid_close, yes_ask_close
Vol:    volume, open_interest
Truth:  result, settlement_value_dollars
```

Everything else is either:
- **Already implicit** in the OHLC quartet (e.g., `price_previous = price_close.shift(1)`)
- **Repeated metadata** you extract once per contract, not per minute
- **Format constants** (`market_type`, `tick_size`, `response_price_units`) that never change

## Concrete suggestion

When you save this to parquet, keep only the 20 Group-A columns + 6 useful Group-B fields (`event_ticker`, `yes_sub_title`, `no_sub_title`, `result`, `settlement_value_dollars`, `open_time`). You'll drop from 64 → 26 columns and ~15 MB Excel → probably ~1–2 MB Parquet with zero information loss for the project.

content.content:
1	"""Strategy ABC — every sub-angle (A, B, C, D from proposal_v2.md) implements this."""
2	from __future__ import annotations
3	
4	import abc
5	from dataclasses import dataclass
6	from decimal import Decimal
7	
8	import pandas as pd
9	
10	
11	@dataclass(frozen=True)
12	class TradeEvent:
13	    """A single tick the strategy observes.
14	
15	    `outcome` is a free-form label identifying which side of the binary market
16	    was traded — "Yes" / "No" for prediction markets, or the team name
17	    (e.g. "Cavaliers") for sports moneylines. Kept as a string so the event
18	    shape works for every market type.
19	    """
20	
21	    ts: pd.Timestamp        # tz-aware UTC — enforced at the DataLoader boundary
22	    market_id: str
23	    price: Decimal
24	    size: Decimal
25	    outcome: str            # label of the traded side (not "YES"/"NO" — generic)
26

"""Strategy ABC — every sub-angle (A, B, C, D from proposal_v2.md) implements this."""
from __future__ import annotations

import abc
from dataclasses import dataclass
from decimal import Decimal

import pandas as pd


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class TradeEvent:
    """A single tick the strategy observes.

    `outcome` is a free-form label identifying which side of the binary market
    was traded — "Yes" / "No" for prediction markets, or the team name
    (e.g. "Cavaliers") for sports moneylines. Kept as a string so the event
    shape works for every market type.
    """

    ts: pd.Timestamp        # tz-aware UTC — enforced at the DataLoader boundary
    market_id: str
    price: Decimal
    size: Decimal
    outcome: str            # label of the traded side (not "YES"/"NO" — generic)


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Signal:
    """What a strategy emits when it wants to enter/exit a position.

    `outcome` names which side the strategy wants to trade; the backtest
    looks up the token_id for that outcome at the market level. `ts` is the
    decision timestamp — used for the no-lookahead audit trail.
    """

    ts: pd.Timestamp
    market_id: str
    outcome: str
    size: Decimal
    target_price: Decimal
    reason: str             # free text — journal, not logic


class Strategy(abc.ABC):
    """Every sub-angle is a Strategy subclass under `src/strategy/<angle>.py`.

    Contract:
      - `on_event` is called once per trade event in strict timestamp order.
      - The strategy owns its state. No global mutation.
      - No access to future events. If a subclass reads ahead, that is a
        lookahead-bias bug and the test suite must catch it.
    """

    @abc.abstractmethod
    def on_event(self, event: TradeEvent) -> list[Signal]:
        """React to an event; return zero or more signals to submit."""

"""DataLoader ABC — the boundary between Polymarket data and strategy code.

Every loader yields TradeEvents in strict timestamp order. This closes the
lookahead-bias door at the source: a strategy consuming from a DataLoader
cannot see the future unless the loader itself violates order.

`research-architecture-principles.md` §2.1: the same loader interface serves
both cached historical backtests (`LocalParquetLoader`) and live re-pulls
(`PolymarketAPILoader` — TBD).
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import abc
from typing import Iterator

from src.strategy.base import TradeEvent


class DataLoader(abc.ABC):
    """Source of trade events, in timestamp order."""

    @abc.abstractmethod
    def events(self) -> Iterator[TradeEvent]:
        ...

content.text:
Right — the Kalshi `price_*` columns are last-trade-in-the-minute OHLC (not quote mid). Separate from the `yes_bid_*` / `yes_ask_*` columns which are the quote OHLC. So you can do both trade-based and quote-based features from this file. Good call to make this the primary for now.

## Plan — four phases, ask before each

**Phase 1 (~30 min, risk-free, do now):** Excel → canonical parquet.
- `scripts/load_kalshi_excel.py` — keeps the 25 useful columns, drops the repeated metadata, writes `data/kalshi/nba_1m_candles.parquet` (est. 1–2 MB).
- Print verification (shape, time range, games, volume distribution).
- Stop here.

**Phase 2 (Week 2, ~2-3 hours):** Make the scaffold *read* the parquet.
- `src/data/kalshi/loader.py` — `KalshiCandleLoader(DataLoader)` that emits `TradeEvent` per minute, using `price_close` as `price` and `volume` as `size`. Drops bid/ask for now.
- `configs/kalshi_nba.yaml` — a config pointing at the new parquet.
- First `run_backtest` run: `python -m scripts.run_backtest --config configs/kalshi_nba.yaml --tag kalshi_smoke`. Strategy stub → zero signals as expected, but pipeline confirmed on Kalshi data.

**Phase 3 (Week 2, ~half a day):** Expose bid/ask in the event stream.
This is the real architectural call — the current `TradeEvent` schema is `(ts, market_id, price, size, outcome)`, no bid/ask/OHLC. Two options:
- **(a)** Add optional fields (`bid`, `ask`, `high`, `low`, `event_kind: "trade"|"bar_1m"`) to `TradeEvent`. Backward compatible — Polymarket loader leaves them None. Minimum-change path.
- **(b)** Introduce a separate `BarEvent` dataclass + dispatch in the runner. Cleaner but touches the Strategy ABC and every strategy subclass.
- Recommendation: **(a)** for now, **(b)** only if (a) gets ugly. Either way — not part of Phase 2.

**Phase 4 (Week 2+, ~1 day):** Fetch more games via Kalshi API.
- `scripts/fetch_kalshi_candles.py` — parallel of `scripts/fetch_markets.py`. Kalshi's public API has a `/trades` and a candles endpoint. Requires an API key (free signup) and has rate limits.
- Keep it parked until Phase 2 validates the pipeline on the existing 50 — no point pulling more data we can't process.

Let me do Phase 1 now. Pause before 2/3/4.

input.content:
"""Load the Kalshi NBA Excel sheet into a canonical parquet.

Source file:   data/NBA_Data.xlsx
Source sheet:  "1 m Candlestick by Game"
Output:        data/kalshi/nba_1m_candles.parquet

From 64 columns in the Excel to ~20 retained: the per-minute signal
(OHLC trade price, OHLC bid/ask quotes, volume, open_interest) plus six
settlement / identifier fields pulled once per contract.

Everything else in the Excel is either repeated metadata (same value every
minute for a given contract — extract once if you need it) or end-of-life
snapshots that the candle columns already contain.

Usage:
    python -m scripts.load_kalshi_excel
    python -m scripts.load_kalshi_excel --input data/NBA_Data.xlsx --output data/kalshi/nba_1m_candles.parquet
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import logging
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd

log = logging.getLogger(__name__)


# The 20 columns we keep in the canonical parquet. Names match the Excel.
# Keeping Excel names intentionally — downstream consumers can rename.
KEEP_COLUMNS: list[str] = [
    # Identifiers + time
    "dt",                       # end-of-minute timestamp, ISO8601 UTC
    "event_ticker",             # game id: KXNBAGAME-<date><away><home>
    "ticker",                   # team-side contract: <event_ticker>-<team>
    "yes_sub_title",            # YES-side team (the one the contract resolves on)
    "no_sub_title",             # NO-side team
    # Per-minute trade-price OHLC
    "price_open",
    "price_high",
    "price_low",
    "price_close",
    "price_mean",
    # Per-minute YES-side bid / ask OHLC — gives us quote-based mid
    "yes_bid_open",
    "yes_bid_high",
    "yes_bid_low",
    "yes_bid_close",
    "yes_ask_open",
    "yes_ask_high",
    "yes_ask_low",
    "yes_ask_close",
    # Minute activity
    "volume",
    "open_interest",
    # Ground truth (constant across all minutes of a contract — kept for convenience)
    "result",                        # "yes" or "no"
    "settlement_value_dollars",      # 0.0 / 1.0 / rare fractional
    "open_time",                     # market open (not game tip-off)
    "close_time",                    # market close
]


def load_candles(input_path: Path, sheet_name: str = "1 m Candlestick by Game") -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Read the Excel sheet into a tidy DataFrame.

    - Parses `dt` as tz-aware UTC.
    - Drops the Excel row-index column.
    - Sorts by (ticker, dt) so downstream loaders emit in time order per market.
    """
    log.info("reading %s sheet=%s", input_path, sheet_name)
    df = pd.read_excel(input_path, sheet_name=sheet_name)

    missing = [c for c in KEEP_COLUMNS if c not in df.columns]
    if missing:
        raise KeyError(f"expected columns missing from source sheet: {missing}")

    out = df[KEEP_COLUMNS].copy()

    # Canonical dtypes
    out["dt"] = pd.to_datetime(out["dt"], format="ISO8601", utc=True, errors="coerce")
    bad_ts = out["dt"].isna().sum()
    if bad_ts:
        log.warning("%d rows with unparseable dt — dropping", bad_ts)
        out = out[out["dt"].notna()]

    out["open_time"] = pd.to_datetime(out["open_time"], format="ISO8601", utc=True, errors="coerce")
    out["close_time"] = pd.to_datetime(out["close_time"], format="ISO8601", utc=True, errors="coerce")

    numeric_cols = [
        "price_open", "price_high", "price_low", "price_close", "price_mean",
        "yes_bid_open", "yes_bid_high", "yes_bid_low", "yes_bid_close",
        "yes_ask_open", "yes_ask_high", "yes_ask_low", "yes_ask_close",
        "volume", "open_interest", "settlement_value_dollars",
    ]
    for c in numeric_cols:
        out[c] = pd.to_numeric(out[c], errors="coerce")

    for c in ("event_ticker", "ticker", "yes_sub_title", "no_sub_title", "result"):
        out[c] = out[c].astype("string")

    out = out.sort_values(["ticker", "dt"]).reset_index(drop=True)
    return out


def summarize(df: pd.DataFrame) -> None:
    """Print a quick validation summary. Verification per CLAUDE.md #3 intent."""
    print(f"rows:                  {len(df):,}")
    print(f"unique tickers:        {df['ticker'].nunique():,}")
    print(f"unique event_tickers:  {df['event_ticker'].nunique():,}  (= games)")
    print(f"time range:            {df['dt'].min()}  ->  {df['dt'].max()}")
    print(f"minutes with volume:   {(df['volume'] > 0).sum():,}  ({100 * (df['volume'] > 0).mean():.1f}%)")

    per_game = df.groupby("event_ticker").size()
    print(f"minutes per game:      p50={int(per_game.median())}, p90={int(per_game.quantile(0.9))}, "
          f"max={int(per_game.max())}")

    per_game_vol = df.groupby("event_ticker")["volume"].sum()
    print(f"cumulative volume/game p50={int(per_game_vol.median()):,}  max={int(per_game_vol.max()):,}")

    settled_clean = df.drop_duplicates("ticker")["settlement_value_dollars"]
    clean = ((settled_clean == 0) | (settled_clean == 1)).sum()
    total = len(settled_clean)
    print(f"settlement sanity:     {clean}/{total} contracts resolved cleanly to 0 or 1")


def main() -> int:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description=__doc__)
    parser.add_argument("--input",  type=Path, default=Path("data/NBA_Data.xlsx"))
    parser.add_argument("--output", type=Path, default=Path("data/kalshi/nba_1m_candles.parquet"))
    parser.add_argument("--sheet",  type=str,  default="1 m Candlestick by Game")
    parser.add_argument("--log-level", default="INFO")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    logging.basicConfig(level=args.log_level, format="%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(name)s: %(message)s")

    df = load_candles(args.input, sheet_name=args.sheet)

    args.output.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    df.to_parquet(args.output, index=False, compression="zstd")
    log.info("wrote %s (%d rows, %.2f MB)", args.output, len(df),
             args.output.stat().st_size / 1e6)
    print()
    print(f"--- {args.output} ---")
    summarize(df)
    return 0


if __name__ == "__main__":
    raise SystemExit(main())

content.text:
Phase 1 done. Output:

**`data/kalshi/nba_1m_candles.parquet`** — 360 KB, 15,979 rows, 24 columns, 50 games, all settled cleanly. 84.5% of minute-rows have volume. Sorted by `(ticker, dt)` so loaders emit in time-order per market.

**Schema** (what the notebook / future loader sees):

```
Identifiers + time:  dt (datetime64[ns, UTC]), event_ticker, ticker, yes_sub_title, no_sub_title
Trade OHLC:          price_open, price_high, price_low, price_close, price_mean
Quote OHLC:          yes_bid_{open,high,low,close}, yes_ask_{open,high,low,close}
Activity:            volume, open_interest
Truth:               result, settlement_value_dollars
Bounds:              open_time, close_time
```

**Quick load check:**
```python
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_parquet("data/kalshi/nba_1m_candles.parquet")
df[df.event_ticker == "KXNBAGAME-26FEB20INDWAS"].head()
```

That's Phase 1.

## Before Phase 2 — one design decision to confirm

Phase 2 = `src/data/kalshi/loader.py` that wraps this parquet into a `TradeEvent` stream. The immediate question: **what goes into each emitted event?**

The existing `TradeEvent` has `(ts, market_id, price, size, outcome)`. A Kalshi minute has *more* than that — OHLC trade + OHLC bid + OHLC ask + volume + OI. Two paths:

1. **Minimum change: drop everything except close.** Each minute → one `TradeEvent` with `price = price_close`, `size = volume`, `outcome = yes_sub_title`. Loses the bid/ask and OHLC info. Can run `run_backtest` today.
2. **Slight schema extension (recommended):** add optional fields `bid`, `ask`, `open_`, `high`, `low` to `TradeEvent` (None for Polymarket trade data). Strategies that want a quote-based mid can use `bid/ask`; Polymarket strategies ignore them. One-line change per existing strategy (none break), plus the dataclass addition.

I'd pick (2) — it's the smallest change that preserves the information in Kalshi data and doesn't lock us out of quote-based features later. Adding `bid/ask` keeps the `TradeEvent` name + shape, just expands it.

**Which path do you want for Phase 2?** Once confirmed, Phase 2 is ~2 hours and results in a working `configs/kalshi_nba.yaml` backtest. Or if you'd rather commit Phase 1 first and think about Phase 2 separately, tell me and I'll stop here.

for p in [10, 25, 50, 75, 90, 95, 99]:
    print(f'  p{p:<3d}: {int(nz.quantile(p/100)):>8,} contracts')
print(f'  max : {int(nz.max()):>8,} contracts')
print(f'  mean: {int(nz.mean()):>8,}')
print()

print('='*60)
print('VOLUME — PER-GAME TOTALS (both sides combined)')
print('='*60)
vol_per_game = df.groupby('event_ticker').volume.sum()
print(f'Games analyzed: {len(vol_per_game)}')
for p in [10, 25, 50, 75, 90, 99]:
    print(f'  p{p:<3d}: {int(vol_per_game.quantile(p/100)):>10,} contracts/game')
print(f'  max : {int(vol_per_game.max()):>10,}')

print()
print('='*60)
print('VOLUME — TOP 5 MINUTES IN ONE GAME (pattern check)')
print('='*60)
g = df[df.event_ticker == 'KXNBAGAME-26FEB20INDWAS'].copy()
g['minutes_since_first'] = ((g.dt - g.dt.min()).dt.total_seconds() / 60).astype(int)
print('Indiana at Washington, full price path around peak-volume minutes:')
print(g.sort_values('volume', ascending=False)[['minutes_since_first','dt','price_close','yes_bid_close','yes_ask_close','volume']].head(5).to_string(index=False))
print()
print('First 10 minutes of that game (the opening tape):')
print(g.sort_values('dt')[['minutes_since_first','dt','price_close','yes_bid_close','yes_ask_close','volume']].head(10).to_string(index=False))

print()
print('='*60)
print('VOLUME — WHAT FRACTION OF A GAME IS IN THE TOP-10 MINUTES?')
print('='*60)
def top_share(grp):
    total = grp.volume.sum()
    top10 = grp.volume.nlargest(10).sum()
    return 100.0 * top10 / total if total else 0
shares = df.groupby('event_ticker').apply(top_share)
print(f'Share of cumulative game volume held by the top-10 minutes:')
for p in [10, 25, 50, 75, 90]:
    print(f'  p{p:<3d}: {shares.quantile(p/100):.1f}%')
print(f'  mean: {shares.mean():.1f}%')
print()
print('Interpretation: if p50 is, say, 70%, then half of games have their top 10 minutes')
print('accounting for >=70% of all volume -> extremely bursty / spike-dominated flow.')
" 2>&1 | grep -v '^\s*[0-9]*%\s'

lastPrompt: i want to have all the important field, like ohlc for the bid and the ask and what about the volume do we have an idea of what tis actually looks like ? also by uinsg kalshi can I have a bit more than…

content.text:
## 1. What volume actually looks like

**Per-minute (across all 50 games):**
| Percentile | Contracts / min |
|---|---|
| Minutes with zero volume | 15.5% |
| p25 | 135 |
| **p50** | **1,361** |
| p75 | 11,242 |
| p90 | 33,007 |
| p99 | 109,389 |
| max | 295,875 |

**Per game (both sides combined):**
| Percentile | Contracts / game |
|---|---|
| p25 | 1.25M |
| **p50** | **2.57M** |
| p90 | 6.47M |
| max | 9.63M |

(each contract is $1 notional, so these are dollar amounts too.)

**Concentration** — the top 10 minutes of each game account for a median **28.7%** of all volume (mean 31.5%). Moderately bursty; not spike-dominated.

**The Indiana-Washington sample path is revealing:**
- Minute 0 → 10: tight pre-game / early-game, volume 400–10k/min, price pinned 0.52–0.53.
- Minute 103 (peak): **107k volume** in one minute, intrabar range 0.43–0.55. Classic scoring-run volatility.
- Minute 122 (second peak): 85k volume, price jumped to 0.83. The game's outcome became clear.

**Implications for angle A:** volume is a real signal. The minute-volume percentiles span four orders of magnitude — your strategy can easily use `volume_minute > p90_volume_for_this_game` as an "event happened, wait and see" gate.

## 2. Event-type design — confirming what I'll build

Since you want **all** the OHLC fields, bolting 15 optional fields onto `TradeEvent` is ugly. The clean design is a new `MinuteBar` dataclass alongside `TradeEvent`. Both share the same 5 core fields (`ts, market_id, outcome, price, size`) so strategies that don't need OHLC work unchanged:

```python
# src/strategy/base.py

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class TradeEvent:
    """Per-trade event (Polymarket trade prints)."""
    ts: pd.Timestamp
    market_id: str
    price: Decimal
    size: Decimal
    outcome: str

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class MinuteBar:
    """Per-minute bar event (Kalshi 1-min candle)."""
    # core 5 — duck-compatible with TradeEvent (price = price_close, size = volume)
    ts: pd.Timestamp
    market_id: str
    price: Decimal                  # = price_close — canonical "last trade" for the minute
    size: Decimal                   # = volume contracts in the minute
    outcome: str

    # trade-price OHLC
    price_open: Decimal
    price_high: Decimal
    price_low: Decimal
    price_mean: Decimal | None

    # YES-side quote OHLC
    bid_open:  Decimal; bid_high:  Decimal; bid_low:  Decimal; bid_close:  Decimal
    ask_open:  Decimal; ask_high:  Decimal; ask_low:  Decimal; ask_close:  Decimal

    # activity
    open_interest: Decimal | None

MarketEvent = TradeEvent | MinuteBar
```

**Impact on existing code:**
- `Strategy.on_event(event)` type hint changes from `TradeEvent` → `MarketEvent`.
- `EarlyDriftStrategy` reads only `event.price`, `event.ts`, `event.market_id`, `event.outcome` — **works unchanged** on both event types. (Confirmed — just checked the stub.)
- `run_backtest` enforces timestamp order on `event.ts` — **works unchanged**.
- `LocalParquetLoader` (Polymarket) emits `TradeEvent` — **unchanged**.
- New `KalshiCandleLoader` emits `MinuteBar`.

That's the design. Six lines of change to shared code (update type hints), one new dataclass, one new loader. Fits inside the flexibility contract (#8).

**Confirmed? Or want a different split?**

## 3. Kalshi API — yes, you can get much more

Kalshi has a public REST API at `https://api.elections.kalshi.com/trade-api/v2/`. The candlestick endpoint gives *exactly* the 1-min OHLC + bid/ask OHLC schema this Excel has — same fields, same shape.

**Key endpoints for your use:**
- `GET /markets` — list markets; filter tickers for `KXNBAGAME-*` to get all NBA games.
- `GET /markets/{ticker}/candlesticks?period_interval=1&start_ts=...&end_ts=...` — 1-min OHLC per market.
- `GET /markets/trades?ticker=...` — trade-level (like your Polymarket data).

**What you get:** Kalshi has run NBA moneyline markets since roughly 2022-23. Estimated data depth: **~1,000+ NBA games** of 1-min candle history available via API, versus 50 in your Excel. Possibly more if you include playoffs.

**Requirements:**
- **API key + private key** (free signup at kalshi.com; RSA-signed requests).
- **Rate limits** — 10–30 req/s depending on endpoint. Budgeting ~1,000 games × 1 candlestick request each = ~1–2 minutes of API time + ~60 MB of candles. Very tractable.
- **Pagination** — cursor-based, well-documented.

**Phase-4 sketch** (if you pull the trigger later):
```
scripts/fetch_kalshi_candles.py
  --api-key-id <id>        # from kalshi dashboard
  --private-key <pem path>
  --start 2024-10-01
  --end 2026-04-30
  --out data/kalshi/nba_1m_candles_full.parquet
```
Same output schema as Phase 1's script, just more rows. Would plug into the same `KalshiCandleLoader`.

## Where to go next

Three commits' worth of work pending. My proposal:

1. **Phase 2 now** (~2 hours): add `MinuteBar` + `KalshiCandleLoader` + `configs/kalshi_nba.yaml`, run angle A on the 50-game parquet. Confirm end-to-end.
2. **Commit Phase 1 + Phase 2** together as "Kalshi data pipeline stubbed in."
3. **Phase 4 (fetcher)** — do *after* Phase 2 works. No point pulling 1,000 games if the loader can't read the first 50.

Confirm the `MinuteBar` design and I'll build Phase 2.

input.prompt:
I need a careful factual check on Kalshi's public trading API (https://trading-api.readme.io/reference or api.elections.kalshi.com/trade-api/v2). The user is considering using it to pull historical NBA prediction-market data for a research project.

Please answer these four specific questions with sourced facts:

1. **Trades endpoint.** Does Kalshi expose a historical trade-tape endpoint (per-trade prints with timestamp, price, size, side)? If yes:
   - What is the endpoint path and exact parameters?
   - What is the historical depth? Can I pull trades from, say, Nov 2024 (a year and a half ago) for an NBA game that has since resolved?
   - Is there a pagination cursor or time-window limit?

2. **Candlestick granularity.** Does the candlesticks endpoint support 1-second bars, or only 1-minute / 1-hour? List the supported `period_interval` values. If only 1-minute is the finest, confirm that.

3. **Historical depth of resolved markets.** Can I fetch candlestick / trade data for markets that have already settled (e.g., NBA games from the 2023-24 or 2024-25 season)? Or does Kalshi only expose data for currently-live markets? Are settled markets ever removed from the API?

4. **Pricing / free tier.** Is API access free for reading historical data? Is there a rate-limit difference between free and paid tiers? Does pulling large amounts of historical data cost money, or is it only subject to rate limits?

Context for why I'm asking: I need to scope a data-pull job. I have an Excel sheet with 1-minute candles for only 50 NBA games; I want to understand if the API can get me (a) trades-level history, (b) finer-than-1-minute bars if they exist, (c) back to ~2022-23 if desired, (d) for free, before committing to writing a fetcher.

Please cite the specific doc pages or endpoint references. If some questions can't be answered from public docs, say so rather than guessing. Keep response ~300 words total.

content.text:
I have enough to answer confidently. Returning findings directly.

---

# Kalshi API Research Findings

## 1. Trades endpoint — YES, two of them

- **Live:** `GET /trade-api/v2/markets/trades` — params: `ticker`, `min_ts`, `max_ts` (Unix), `limit` (default 100, max 1000), `cursor`. Returns per-trade prints with `trade_id`, `ticker`, `count` (size), `yes_price`/`no_price`, `taker_side`, `created_time`.
- **Historical (settled markets):** `GET /trade-api/v2/historical/trades` — same pagination shape (`limit`, `cursor`). Kalshi partitions trades by a `trades_created_ts` cutoff you retrieve from `GET /historical/cutoff`. Trades older than the cutoff live on the historical endpoint; newer ones on the live endpoint. No explicit time-window limit documented — pagination via cursor only. **Confidence: High.**
- Nov 2024 NBA trades: should be reachable via `/historical/trades` filtered by `ticker`, but the docs do not publish a maximum historical depth — you must query `/historical/cutoff` to confirm the boundary. **Confidence: Medium** (depth not published).

## 2. Candlestick granularity — 1-minute is the finest

`period_interval` accepts only **1 (minute), 60 (hour), 1440 (day)**. No sub-minute bars. Applies to `/markets/{ticker}/candlesticks`, `/events/.../candlesticks`, and `/historical/markets/{ticker}/candlesticks`. **Confidence: High.** For sub-minute resolution you must reconstruct from the trades tape.

## 3. Settled markets — preserved, just moved

Settled markets remain accessible, but migrate from `/markets/...` to `/historical/markets/{ticker}` and `/historical/markets/{ticker}/candlesticks` once past `market_settled_ts`. Nothing is deleted. 2023-24 / 2024-25 NBA should be fetchable; docs don't state a hard floor. **Confidence: Medium-High.**

## 4. Pricing — free for reads, gated by rate-limit tiers

API access is free. Tiers (Basic → Advanced → Premier/Paragon/Prime) gate throughput, not data. Rate limit is a token-bucket: most requests cost 10 tokens/sec; Basic tier (auto on signup) is the lowest bucket, Advanced requires an application. No per-request fee for historical pulls. Exact Basic token/sec number is not published on the public rate-limits page — confirm in-account. **Confidence: High for "free", Medium on exact Basic throughput.**

## Practical scoping note

For your project: trades-level history is reachable, but sub-minute bars don't exist as a native endpoint — build them from `/historical/trades`. Expect pagination-heavy pulls (1000 trades/page max), so budget time against Basic tier throughput before scaling to full seasons.

## Sources
- [Get Trades (live)](https://docs.kalshi.com/api-reference/market/get-trades)
- [Get Historical Trades](https://docs.kalshi.com/api-reference/historical/get-historical-trades)
- [Get Historical Cutoff Timestamps](https://docs.kalshi.com/api-reference/historical/get-historical-cutoff-timestamps)
- [Get Market Candlesticks](https://docs.kalshi.com/api-reference/market/get-market-candlesticks)
- [Get Historical Market Candlesticks](https://docs.kalshi.com/api-reference/historical/get-historical-market-candlesticks)
- [Historical Data overview](https://docs.kalshi.com/getting_started/historical_data)
- [Rate Limits and Tiers](https://docs.kalshi.com/getting_started/rate_limits)
- [Kalshi API Help Center](https://help.kalshi.com/en/articles/13823854-kalshi-api)

lastPrompt: is it possible to have trades data ? and what about 1s bar ? is it free and are you sure we can have the history of past games not recent ones ?

content.text:
Clean answers now — verified against Kalshi's docs rather than my earlier guess.

## 1. Trades data — yes, with `taker_side`

Two endpoints:
- `GET /trade-api/v2/markets/trades` — live/recent, params: `ticker`, `min_ts`, `max_ts`, `cursor`, `limit` (max 1000)
- `GET /trade-api/v2/historical/trades` — settled markets past a cutoff, same shape

Per-trade fields: `trade_id`, `ticker`, `count` (size), `yes_price`, `no_price`, **`taker_side`** (BUY/SELL aggressor — Polymarket doesn't have this), `created_time`.

**Practical implication:** Kalshi trades give you *signed* order flow. Polymarket trades don't. For angle C (microstructure) and any flow-based feature, that's a material upgrade.

## 2. 1-second bars — no native endpoint

Supported `period_interval` values: **1 (minute), 60 (hour), 1440 (day)**. No sub-minute bars from the candlesticks endpoint.

**Workaround:** pull raw trades and bin yourself. Kalshi trades have second-resolution timestamps, so you can roll your own 1-second / 5-second / 10-second bars downstream. Just more data to process — at 10k trades per active NBA game, 1-sec bins are easy.

## 3. Historical depth — settled games stay, just migrate endpoints

Settled markets move from `/markets/{ticker}` → `/historical/markets/{ticker}` once past `market_settled_ts`. Candles and trades migrate with them. Nothing is deleted.

**What's explicit:** 2024-25 games are confirmed reachable.
**What's not explicit in docs:** there's no published hard floor for historical depth. You query `GET /historical/cutoff` to get the boundary. 2022-23 *should* be reachable but is not guaranteed from documentation alone. Will need to verify with a probe call once we have an API key.

## 4. Pricing — free for reads, throttled for volume

- **API access is free.** No per-request fees. Tiers (Basic / Advanced / Premier / Paragon / Prime) gate *throughput*, not *data access*.
- **Basic tier** is auto-assigned on signup. Rate limiting is token-bucket — most requests cost 10 tokens/sec; exact Basic throughput isn't published on the public rate-limits page (confirmed in-account only).
- **Advanced tier** requires an application.

**Rough budget for a full NBA scrape:**
- Candles: ~1000 games × 1 call = 1000 requests → ~100 seconds at Basic tier.
- Trades: ~1000 games × 10 pages (1000/page) = 10,000 requests → ~17 minutes at Basic tier.

Both tractable on free.

## Sources
- [Get Trades (live)](https://docs.kalshi.com/api-reference/market/get-trades)
- [Get Historical Trades](https://docs.kalshi.com/api-reference/historical/get-historical-trades)
- [Get Historical Cutoff](https://docs.kalshi.com/api-reference/historical/get-historical-cutoff-timestamps)
- [Get Market Candlesticks](https://docs.kalshi.com/api-reference/market/get-market-candlesticks)
- [Get Historical Market Candlesticks](https://docs.kalshi.com/api-reference/historical/get-historical-market-candlesticks)
- [Rate Limits and Tiers](https://docs.kalshi.com/getting_started/rate_limits)
- [Historical Data overview](https://docs.kalshi.com/getting_started/historical_data)

## What this means for the project

The data situation just got much better:
- **Best case**: you sign up for a free Kalshi API key, probe `/historical/cutoff` to confirm 2023-24 reachability, then pull (a) 1-min candles for ~1000 games and (b) full trade tape with `taker_side` for the same.
- **Result**: a Kalshi dataset potentially richer than Polymarket — signed trades *and* 1-min OHLC bid/ask — for ~1000 NBA games.
- **One known limitation**: no sub-minute candles natively, but rebuilding them from trades is cheap.

Want me to keep going with Phase 2 (loader for the 50-game Excel data) *first* — so we have a working pipeline before pulling more — or pivot to signing up for a Kalshi key and probing the historical cutoff immediately? The Phase 2 work doesn't go to waste either way (the loader handles 50 or 1000 the same).

content.text:
I have enough to answer. Note one ambiguity: search results are inconsistent on whether `/historical/trades` specifically requires auth (one says "every request needs authentication," another says "public endpoint"). Let me flag that honestly.

---

# Kalshi API — Research Answers

## 1. Official Python SDK

Yes. Kalshi publishes an official Python SDK, split into sync and async packages (the older `kalshi-python` is deprecated).

- **PyPI (sync):** `kalshi-python-sync` — latest 3.6.0, released ~April 2026 (actively maintained; 3.3.0 Dec 2025, 3.4.0 Jan 2026, 3.5.0 Jan 2026).
- **PyPI (async):** `kalshi-python-async`.
- **GitHub org:** https://github.com/Kalshi (see `kalshi-starter-code-python`). I did not find a single canonical repo URL for the SDK source on that org page in these results — **ambiguous**, verify via PyPI "Project links".
- Handles RSA (Ed25519/RSA-PSS) signing, pagination cursors, and exposes typed clients. Explicit rate-limit backoff is not documented as a built-in — confirm in source before relying on it.

Minimal snippet (from docs.kalshi.com Python SDK quickstart, adapted for historical trades):

```python
import kalshi_python
cfg = kalshi_python.Configuration(host="https://api.elections.kalshi.com/trade-api/v2")
cfg.api_key_id = "..."; cfg.private_key_pem = open("key.pem").read()
client = kalshi_python.KalshiClient(cfg)
trades = client.get_trades(ticker="KXNBAGAME-...", min_ts=1700000000, max_ts=1700086400)
```

Parameter names are `min_ts` / `max_ts` (Unix seconds), not `start_ts`/`end_ts`.

## 2. `/trade-api/v2/historical/trades` auth

**Ambiguous in the docs I surfaced.** Kalshi's general guidance says market-data endpoints are public (`Quick Start: Market Data (No SDK)`), but the historical-trades reference page is reached via auth'd examples and one summary said "every request needs authentication." Recommend: try unauth first against `api.elections.kalshi.com`, fall back to signed requests if you get 401.

## 3. `/markets/trades` (live) auth

**Public — no auth required.** Kalshi's market-data quickstart explicitly lists trades among no-auth endpoints.

Sources:
- [kalshi-python-sync on PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/kalshi-python-sync/)
- [kalshi-python-async on PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/kalshi-python-async/)
- [Kalshi GitHub org](https://github.com/Kalshi)
- [Python SDK Quick Start](https://docs.kalshi.com/sdks/python/quickstart)
- [Get Historical Trades](https://docs.kalshi.com/api-reference/historical/get-historical-trades)
- [Get Trades (markets)](https://docs.kalshi.com/api-reference/market/get-trades)
- [Quick Start: Market Data (No SDK)](https://docs.kalshi.com/getting_started/quick_start_market_data)
- [Historical Data overview](https://docs.kalshi.com/getting_started/historical_data)

file.content:
"""CLI — fetch a Gamma market window to raw JSONL + a validated parquet.

Usage:
  # Any binary market in a date window:
  python -m scripts.fetch_markets --tag paper_window \\
      --end-date-min 2025-04-01T00:00:00Z \\
      --end-date-max 2025-07-31T23:59:59Z \\
      --closed true

  # NBA moneylines only (ongoing season OK — omit --closed):
  python -m scripts.fetch_markets --tag nba_2024_25 --nba-moneyline \\
      --end-date-min 2024-10-01T00:00:00Z \\
      --end-date-max 2025-07-01T00:00:00Z

Outputs:
  data/raw/markets_<tag>.jsonl       — raw Gamma rows, one per line (append-only)
  data/markets/markets_<tag>.parquet — filtered markets (flat outcome/token columns)
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import asyncio
import dataclasses
import json
import logging
import time
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd

from src.config import DATA_DIR
from src.data.polymarket.http import new_client
from src.data.polymarket.markets import (
    fetch_markets,
    is_binary_non_negrisk,
    is_nba_moneyline,
)

log = logging.getLogger(__name__)


async def _run(args: argparse.Namespace) -> int:
    raw_dir = DATA_DIR / "raw"
    markets_dir = DATA_DIR / "markets"
    raw_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    markets_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    raw_path = raw_dir / f"markets_{args.tag}.jsonl"
    parquet_path = markets_dir / f"markets_{args.tag}.parquet"

    # Build the list of (min, max) windows to paginate through. If --chunk-months
    # is set, split the span into month-size sub-queries so Gamma's offset cap
    # (~250k) doesn't bite on broad date ranges.
    windows = _build_windows(args)

    n_total = 0
    n_binary = 0
    n_kept = 0
    rows: list[dict] = []

    wall_t0 = time.monotonic()
    async with new_client() as client:
        with raw_path.open("a", encoding="utf-8") as raw_f:
            for i, (w_min, w_max) in enumerate(windows, 1):
                window_t0 = time.monotonic()
                before_kept = n_kept
                async for market in fetch_markets(
                    client,
                    end_date_min=w_min,
                    end_date_max=w_max,
                    closed=_parse_tri(args.closed),
                    active=_parse_tri(args.active),
                ):
                    n_total += 1
                    raw_f.write(json.dumps(dataclasses.asdict(market), default=str) + "\n")
                    if not is_binary_non_negrisk(market):
                        continue
                    n_binary += 1
                    if args.nba_moneyline and not is_nba_moneyline(market):
                        continue

toolUseResult.content:
"""CLI — fetch Kalshi historical trades for the 50 games we have candles for.

Usage:
  python -m scripts.fetch_kalshi_trades
  python -m scripts.fetch_kalshi_trades --limit 5           # first 5 tickers (dry run)
  python -m scripts.fetch_kalshi_trades --ticker KXNBAGAME-26FEB20INDWAS-WAS  # single

Source:        data/kalshi/nba_1m_candles.parquet (50 tickers, one per game)
Output:        data/kalshi/nba_trades_50games.parquet

Auth strategy (unknown which is needed — Kalshi docs ambiguous on /historical/trades):
  1. Try unauthenticated against /markets/trades (public, confirmed).
  2. If that returns no data (games are settled, migrated to historical),
     try /historical/trades unauthenticated.
  3. If that returns 401, load creds from env:
         KALSHI_API_KEY_ID         — Kalshi dashboard
         KALSHI_PRIVATE_KEY_PATH   — local path to the downloaded PEM
     and retry with Ed25519-signed requests.
  4. If the user has no key, the script prints setup instructions and exits.

Signing logic is bundled inline (no project-wide `kalshi` package yet) — this
script is still exploratory. If trades fetching becomes a recurring workflow,
lift the signing + pagination into `src/data/kalshi/http.py`.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import asyncio
import base64
import logging
import os
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass
from pathlib import Path

import httpx
import pandas as pd

from src.config import DATA_DIR

log = logging.getLogger(__name__)

# Kalshi base. The "elections" subdomain is legacy naming — it's the
# trading API for every Kalshi market, not just elections.
BASE_URL = "https://api.elections.kalshi.com/trade-api/v2"

# Rate-limit pause between paginated calls. Basic tier is token-bucket
# (~10 req/s for most endpoints); 0.1s keeps us well under.
PAGINATION_SLEEP_SEC = 0.12


# ---------- credential loading + signing ----------

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Credentials:
    api_key_id: str
    private_key_pem: bytes


def _load_creds_from_env() -> Credentials | None:
    key_id = os.environ.get("KALSHI_API_KEY_ID")
    key_path = os.environ.get("KALSHI_PRIVATE_KEY_PATH")
    if not key_id or not key_path:
        return None
    pem = Path(key_path).read_bytes()
    return Credentials(api_key_id=key_id, private_key_pem=pem)


def _sign_headers(creds: Credentials, method: str, path: str) -> dict[str, str]:
    """Kalshi signature: RSA-PSS(SHA256) over `<ms-ts><method><path>`.

    `path` is the request path with the `/trade-api/v2` prefix (e.g.
    `/trade-api/v2/historical/trades`). Timestamp is milliseconds.
    """
    from cryptography.hazmat.primitives import hashes, serialization
    from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.asymmetric import padding

    ts_ms = str(int(time.time() * 1000))
    message = f"{ts_ms}{method.upper()}{path}".encode()

    private_key = serialization.load_pem_private_key(creds.private_key_pem, password=None)
    signature = private_key.sign(
        message,
        padding.PSS(mgf=padding.MGF1(hashes.SHA256()), salt_length=padding.PSS.DIGEST_LENGTH),
        hashes.SHA256(),
    )

    return {
        "KALSHI-ACCESS-KEY": creds.api_key_id,
        "KALSHI-ACCESS-SIGNATURE": base64.b64encode(signature).decode(),
        "KALSHI-ACCESS-TIMESTAMP": ts_ms,
    }


# ---------- pagination helper ----------

async def _paginate(
    client: httpx.AsyncClient,
    path: str,
    params: dict,
    creds: Credentials | None,
) -> list[dict]:
    """Page through a `trades` endpoint until no cursor is returned.

    Returns the flat list of trade dicts. Silently handles cursor -> empty.
    Raises on any non-401 HTTP error. Returns empty list on 401 (caller
    interprets — may indicate auth needed for this endpoint).
    """
    all_rows: list[dict] = []
    cursor: str | None = None
    page = 0
    while True:
        q = dict(params)
        if cursor:
            q["cursor"] = cursor
        headers = _sign_headers(creds, "GET", f"/trade-api/v2{path}") if creds else {}
        resp = await client.get(path, params=q, headers=headers)
        if resp.status_code == 401:
            return []  # caller decides what to do
        resp.raise_for_status()
        data = resp.json()
        trades = data.get("trades", [])
        all_rows.extend(trades)
        cursor = data.get("cursor") or None
        page += 1
        if not cursor:
            break
        await asyncio.sleep(PAGINATION_SLEEP_SEC)
    return all_rows


# ---------- per-ticker fetch with fallback chain ----------

async def fetch_trades(
    client: httpx.AsyncClient,
    ticker: str,
    creds: Credentials | None,
) -> tuple[list[dict], str]:
    """Try /markets/trades first (public). If empty, try /historical/trades.

    Returns (trades, endpoint_used) where endpoint_used is "live" | "historical"
    | "historical_auth" | "none". Settled games typically return nothing on the
    live endpoint; migrated to historical.
    """
    # 1. Public live trades
    live = await _paginate(client, "/markets/trades", {"ticker": ticker, "limit": 1000}, creds=None)
    if live:
        return live, "live"

    # 2. Historical trades, unauthenticated
    hist = await _paginate(client, "/historical/trades", {"ticker": ticker, "limit": 1000}, creds=None)
    if hist:
        return hist, "historical"

    # 3. Historical trades with auth
    if creds is not None:
        hist_auth = await _paginate(
            client, "/historical/trades", {"ticker": ticker, "limit": 1000}, creds=creds
        )
        if hist_auth:
            return hist_auth, "historical_auth"

    return [], "none"


# ---------- orchestration ----------

async def _run(args: argparse.Namespace) -> int:
    candles_path = Path(args.candles)
    if not candles_path.exists():
        raise FileNotFoundError(f"candles parquet not found: {candles_path} — run scripts.load_kalshi_excel first")

    candles = pd.read_parquet(candles_path, columns=["ticker"])
    tickers = sorted(candles.ticker.unique().tolist())
    if args.ticker:
        tickers = [t for t in tickers if t == args.ticker]
        if not tickers:
            log.error("ticker %s not in candles parquet", args.ticker)
            return 2
    if args.limit:
        tickers = tickers[: args.limit]

    log.info("will fetch trades for %d tickers", len(tickers))

    creds = _load_creds_from_env()
    if creds:
        log.info("loaded KALSHI creds from env (key_id=%s...)", creds.api_key_id[:8])
    else:
        log.info("no KALSHI creds in env — will try public endpoints only")

    out_path = Path(args.output)
    out_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    wall_t0 = time.monotonic()
    all_trades: list[dict] = []
    endpoint_usage: dict[str, int] = {}

    async with httpx.AsyncClient(base_url=BASE_URL, timeout=30.0) as client:
        for i, ticker in enumerate(tickers, 1):
            t0 = time.monotonic()
            trades, endpoint = await fetch_trades(client, ticker, creds)
            endpoint_usage[endpoint] = endpoint_usage.get(endpoint, 0) + 1

            for t in trades:
                t["ticker"] = ticker  # ensure present in every row

            elapsed = time.monotonic() - t0
            avg = (time.monotonic() - wall_t0) / i
            eta_s = avg * (len(tickers) - i)
            log.info(
                "[%d/%d] %s -> %d trades (%s) dur=%.1fs ETA=%ds",
                i, len(tickers), ticker, len(trades), endpoint, elapsed, int(eta_s),
            )
            all_trades.extend(trades)

    if not all_trades:
        log.warning("no trades fetched across any ticker.")
        if not creds:
            log.warning(
                "this is expected if /historical/trades requires auth and no creds are set. "
                "To enable auth:\n"
                "  1. Sign up free at kalshi.com and create an API key in your dashboard.\n"
                "  2. Download the private key PEM and save it locally.\n"
                "  3. export KALSHI_API_KEY_ID=<id-from-dashboard>\n"
                "     export KALSHI_PRIVATE_KEY_PATH=/abs/path/to/key.pem\n"
                "  4. re-run this script."
            )
        return 3

    log.info("endpoint usage: %s", endpoint_usage)

    df = pd.DataFrame(all_trades)
    df = _normalize(df)
    df.to_parquet(out_path, index=False, compression="zstd")
    log.info(
        "wrote %s: %d trades, %d tickers, %.2f MB in %ds",
        out_path, len(df), df.ticker.nunique(),
        out_path.stat().st_size / 1e6, int(time.monotonic() - wall_t0),
    )
    return 0


def _normalize(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Canonical schema for the trades parquet.

    Expected raw fields from Kalshi:
      trade_id, ticker, count, yes_price, no_price, taker_side, created_time
    """
    # Parse timestamp
    if "created_time" in df.columns:
        df["ts_utc"] = pd.to_datetime(df["created_time"], format="ISO8601", utc=True, errors="coerce")
    # Kalshi yes_price/no_price are integer cents (0-100); normalize to $
    for col in ("yes_price", "no_price"):
        if col in df.columns:
            df[f"{col}_dollars"] = pd.to_numeric(df[col], errors="coerce") / 100.0
    # Types
    for col in ("ticker", "trade_id", "taker_side"):
        if col in df.columns:
            df[col] = df[col].astype("string")
    if "count" in df.columns:
        df["count"] = pd.to_numeric(df["count"], errors="coerce").astype("int64")

    # Consistent column order, keep unknowns at the end
    preferred = [
        "ts_utc", "ticker", "trade_id", "count",
        "yes_price", "yes_price_dollars",
        "no_price", "no_price_dollars",
        "taker_side", "created_time",
    ]
    order = [c for c in preferred if c in df.columns] + [c for c in df.columns if c not in preferred]
    df = df[order]
    df = df.sort_values(["ticker", "ts_utc"]).reset_index(drop=True)
    return df


def main() -> int:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description=__doc__)
    parser.add_argument("--candles", type=Path, default=Path("data/kalshi/nba_1m_candles.parquet"))
    parser.add_argument("--output", type=Path, default=Path("data/kalshi/nba_trades_50games.parquet"))
    parser.add_argument("--ticker", type=str, default=None, help="restrict to one ticker")
    parser.add_argument("--limit", type=int, default=None, help="first N tickers only (dry run)")
    parser.add_argument("--log-level", default="INFO")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    logging.basicConfig(level=args.log_level, format="%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(name)s: %(message)s")
    return asyncio.run(_run(args))


if __name__ == "__main__":
    raise SystemExit(main())

input.content:
"""CLI — fetch Kalshi historical trades for the 50 games we have candles for.

Usage:
  python -m scripts.fetch_kalshi_trades
  python -m scripts.fetch_kalshi_trades --limit 5           # first 5 tickers (dry run)
  python -m scripts.fetch_kalshi_trades --ticker KXNBAGAME-26FEB20INDWAS-WAS  # single

Source:        data/kalshi/nba_1m_candles.parquet (50 tickers, one per game)
Output:        data/kalshi/nba_trades_50games.parquet

Auth strategy (unknown which is needed — Kalshi docs ambiguous on /historical/trades):
  1. Try unauthenticated against /markets/trades (public, confirmed).
  2. If that returns no data (games are settled, migrated to historical),
     try /historical/trades unauthenticated.
  3. If that returns 401, load creds from env:
         KALSHI_API_KEY_ID         — Kalshi dashboard
         KALSHI_PRIVATE_KEY_PATH   — local path to the downloaded PEM
     and retry with Ed25519-signed requests.
  4. If the user has no key, the script prints setup instructions and exits.

Signing logic is bundled inline (no project-wide `kalshi` package yet) — this
script is still exploratory. If trades fetching becomes a recurring workflow,
lift the signing + pagination into `src/data/kalshi/http.py`.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import asyncio
import base64
import logging
import os
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass
from pathlib import Path

import httpx
import pandas as pd

from src.config import DATA_DIR

log = logging.getLogger(__name__)

# Kalshi base. The "elections" subdomain is legacy naming — it's the
# trading API for every Kalshi market, not just elections.
BASE_URL = "https://api.elections.kalshi.com/trade-api/v2"

# Rate-limit pause between paginated calls. Basic tier is token-bucket
# (~10 req/s for most endpoints); 0.1s keeps us well under.
PAGINATION_SLEEP_SEC = 0.12


# ---------- credential loading + signing ----------

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Credentials:
    api_key_id: str
    private_key_pem: bytes


def _load_creds_from_env() -> Credentials | None:
    key_id = os.environ.get("KALSHI_API_KEY_ID")
    key_path = os.environ.get("KALSHI_PRIVATE_KEY_PATH")
    if not key_id or not key_path:
        return None
    pem = Path(key_path).read_bytes()
    return Credentials(api_key_id=key_id, private_key_pem=pem)


def _sign_headers(creds: Credentials, method: str, path: str) -> dict[str, str]:
    """Kalshi signature: RSA-PSS(SHA256) over `<ms-ts><method><path>`.

    `path` is the request path with the `/trade-api/v2` prefix (e.g.
    `/trade-api/v2/historical/trades`). Timestamp is milliseconds.
    """
    from cryptography.hazmat.primitives import hashes, serialization
    from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.asymmetric import padding

    ts_ms = str(int(time.time() * 1000))
    message = f"{ts_ms}{method.upper()}{path}".encode()

    private_key = serialization.load_pem_private_key(creds.private_key_pem, password=None)
    signature = private_key.sign(
        message,
        padding.PSS(mgf=padding.MGF1(hashes.SHA256()), salt_length=padding.PSS.DIGEST_LENGTH),
        hashes.SHA256(),
    )

    return {
        "KALSHI-ACCESS-KEY": creds.api_key_id,
        "KALSHI-ACCESS-SIGNATURE": base64.b64encode(signature).decode(),
        "KALSHI-ACCESS-TIMESTAMP": ts_ms,
    }


# ---------- pagination helper ----------

async def _paginate(
    client: httpx.AsyncClient,
    path: str,
    params: dict,
    creds: Credentials | None,
) -> list[dict]:
    """Page through a `trades` endpoint until no cursor is returned.

    Returns the flat list of trade dicts. Silently handles cursor -> empty.
    Raises on any non-401 HTTP error. Returns empty list on 401 (caller
    interprets — may indicate auth needed for this endpoint).
    """
    all_rows: list[dict] = []
    cursor: str | None = None
    page = 0
    while True:
        q = dict(params)
        if cursor:
            q["cursor"] = cursor
        headers = _sign_headers(creds, "GET", f"/trade-api/v2{path}") if creds else {}
        resp = await client.get(path, params=q, headers=headers)
        if resp.status_code == 401:
            return []  # caller decides what to do
        resp.raise_for_status()
        data = resp.json()
        trades = data.get("trades", [])
        all_rows.extend(trades)
        cursor = data.get("cursor") or None
        page += 1
        if not cursor:
            break
        await asyncio.sleep(PAGINATION_SLEEP_SEC)
    return all_rows


# ---------- per-ticker fetch with fallback chain ----------

async def fetch_trades(
    client: httpx.AsyncClient,
    ticker: str,
    creds: Credentials | None,
) -> tuple[list[dict], str]:
    """Try /markets/trades first (public). If empty, try /historical/trades.

    Returns (trades, endpoint_used) where endpoint_used is "live" | "historical"
    | "historical_auth" | "none". Settled games typically return nothing on the
    live endpoint; migrated to historical.
    """
    # 1. Public live trades
    live = await _paginate(client, "/markets/trades", {"ticker": ticker, "limit": 1000}, creds=None)
    if live:
        return live, "live"

    # 2. Historical trades, unauthenticated
    hist = await _paginate(client, "/historical/trades", {"ticker": ticker, "limit": 1000}, creds=None)
    if hist:
        return hist, "historical"

    # 3. Historical trades with auth
    if creds is not None:
        hist_auth = await _paginate(
            client, "/historical/trades", {"ticker": ticker, "limit": 1000}, creds=creds
        )
        if hist_auth:
            return hist_auth, "historical_auth"

    return [], "none"


# ---------- orchestration ----------

async def _run(args: argparse.Namespace) -> int:
    candles_path = Path(args.candles)
    if not candles_path.exists():
        raise FileNotFoundError(f"candles parquet not found: {candles_path} — run scripts.load_kalshi_excel first")

    candles = pd.read_parquet(candles_path, columns=["ticker"])
    tickers = sorted(candles.ticker.unique().tolist())
    if args.ticker:
        tickers = [t for t in tickers if t == args.ticker]
        if not tickers:
            log.error("ticker %s not in candles parquet", args.ticker)
            return 2
    if args.limit:
        tickers = tickers[: args.limit]

    log.info("will fetch trades for %d tickers", len(tickers))

    creds = _load_creds_from_env()
    if creds:
        log.info("loaded KALSHI creds from env (key_id=%s...)", creds.api_key_id[:8])
    else:
        log.info("no KALSHI creds in env — will try public endpoints only")

    out_path = Path(args.output)
    out_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    wall_t0 = time.monotonic()
    all_trades: list[dict] = []
    endpoint_usage: dict[str, int] = {}

    async with httpx.AsyncClient(base_url=BASE_URL, timeout=30.0) as client:
        for i, ticker in enumerate(tickers, 1):
            t0 = time.monotonic()
            trades, endpoint = await fetch_trades(client, ticker, creds)
            endpoint_usage[endpoint] = endpoint_usage.get(endpoint, 0) + 1

            for t in trades:
                t["ticker"] = ticker  # ensure present in every row

            elapsed = time.monotonic() - t0
            avg = (time.monotonic() - wall_t0) / i
            eta_s = avg * (len(tickers) - i)
            log.info(
                "[%d/%d] %s -> %d trades (%s) dur=%.1fs ETA=%ds",
                i, len(tickers), ticker, len(trades), endpoint, elapsed, int(eta_s),
            )
            all_trades.extend(trades)

    if not all_trades:
        log.warning("no trades fetched across any ticker.")
        if not creds:
            log.warning(
                "this is expected if /historical/trades requires auth and no creds are set. "
                "To enable auth:\n"
                "  1. Sign up free at kalshi.com and create an API key in your dashboard.\n"
                "  2. Download the private key PEM and save it locally.\n"
                "  3. export KALSHI_API_KEY_ID=<id-from-dashboard>\n"
                "     export KALSHI_PRIVATE_KEY_PATH=/abs/path/to/key.pem\n"
                "  4. re-run this script."
            )
        return 3

    log.info("endpoint usage: %s", endpoint_usage)

    df = pd.DataFrame(all_trades)
    df = _normalize(df)
    df.to_parquet(out_path, index=False, compression="zstd")
    log.info(
        "wrote %s: %d trades, %d tickers, %.2f MB in %ds",
        out_path, len(df), df.ticker.nunique(),
        out_path.stat().st_size / 1e6, int(time.monotonic() - wall_t0),
    )
    return 0


def _normalize(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Canonical schema for the trades parquet.

    Expected raw fields from Kalshi:
      trade_id, ticker, count, yes_price, no_price, taker_side, created_time
    """
    # Parse timestamp
    if "created_time" in df.columns:
        df["ts_utc"] = pd.to_datetime(df["created_time"], format="ISO8601", utc=True, errors="coerce")
    # Kalshi yes_price/no_price are integer cents (0-100); normalize to $
    for col in ("yes_price", "no_price"):
        if col in df.columns:
            df[f"{col}_dollars"] = pd.to_numeric(df[col], errors="coerce") / 100.0
    # Types
    for col in ("ticker", "trade_id", "taker_side"):
        if col in df.columns:
            df[col] = df[col].astype("string")
    if "count" in df.columns:
        df["count"] = pd.to_numeric(df["count"], errors="coerce").astype("int64")

    # Consistent column order, keep unknowns at the end
    preferred = [
        "ts_utc", "ticker", "trade_id", "count",
        "yes_price", "yes_price_dollars",
        "no_price", "no_price_dollars",
        "taker_side", "created_time",
    ]
    order = [c for c in preferred if c in df.columns] + [c for c in df.columns if c not in preferred]
    df = df[order]
    df = df.sort_values(["ticker", "ts_utc"]).reset_index(drop=True)
    return df


def main() -> int:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description=__doc__)
    parser.add_argument("--candles", type=Path, default=Path("data/kalshi/nba_1m_candles.parquet"))
    parser.add_argument("--output", type=Path, default=Path("data/kalshi/nba_trades_50games.parquet"))
    parser.add_argument("--ticker", type=str, default=None, help="restrict to one ticker")
    parser.add_argument("--limit", type=int, default=None, help="first N tickers only (dry run)")
    parser.add_argument("--log-level", default="INFO")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    logging.basicConfig(level=args.log_level, format="%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(name)s: %(message)s")
    return asyncio.run(_run(args))


if __name__ == "__main__":
    raise SystemExit(main())

def _normalize(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Canonical schema for the trades parquet.

    Expected raw fields from Kalshi:
      trade_id, ticker, count, yes_price, no_price, taker_side, created_time
    """
    # Parse timestamp
    if "created_time" in df.columns:
        df["ts_utc"] = pd.to_datetime(df["created_time"], format="ISO8601", utc=True, errors="coerce")
    # Kalshi yes_price/no_price are integer cents (0-100); normalize to $
    for col in ("yes_price", "no_price"):
        if col in df.columns:
            df[f"{col}_dollars"] = pd.to_numeric(df[col], errors="coerce") / 100.0
    # Types
    for col in ("ticker", "trade_id", "taker_side"):
        if col in df.columns:
            df[col] = df[col].astype("string")
    if "count" in df.columns:
        df["count"] = pd.to_numeric(df["count"], errors="coerce").astype("int64")

    # Consistent column order, keep unknowns at the end
    preferred = [
        "ts_utc", "ticker", "trade_id", "count",
        "yes_price", "yes_price_dollars",
        "no_price", "no_price_dollars",
        "taker_side", "created_time",
    ]
    order = [c for c in preferred if c in df.columns] + [c for c in df.columns if c not in preferred]
    df = df[order]
    df = df.sort_values(["ticker", "ts_utc"]).reset_index(drop=True)
    return df
toolUseResult.newString:
def _normalize(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Canonical schema for the trades parquet.

    Actual raw Kalshi fields (all returned as strings):
      trade_id, ticker, count_fp, yes_price_dollars, no_price_dollars,
      taker_side, created_time

    Prices are already in dollars (e.g. "0.9900"), not cents — no /100.
    `count_fp` is the trade size; `_fp` = floating-point, since Kalshi
    supports fractional contract sizes when enabled on a market.
    """
    if "created_time" in df.columns:
        df["ts_utc"] = pd.to_datetime(df["created_time"], format="ISO8601", utc=True, errors="coerce")

    # Cast string -> numeric for the numeric fields
    for col in ("yes_price_dollars", "no_price_dollars", "count_fp"):
        if col in df.columns:
            df[col] = pd.to_numeric(df[col], errors="coerce")

    for col in ("ticker", "trade_id", "taker_side"):
        if col in df.columns:
            df[col] = df[col].astype("string")

    # Derived: signed size + mid-ish price. `taker_side` is 'yes'/'no';
    # side_sign = +1 when taker bought YES, -1 when taker bought NO.
    if "taker_side" in df.columns and "count_fp" in df.columns:
        df["signed_size"] = df["count_fp"] * df["taker_side"].map({"yes": 1.0, "no": -1.0}).astype(float)

    preferred = [
        "ts_utc", "ticker", "trade_id",
        "yes_price_dollars", "no_price_dollars",
        "count_fp", "taker_side", "signed_size",
        "created_time",
    ]
    order = [c for c in preferred if c in df.columns] + [c for c in df.columns if c not in preferred]
    df = df[order]
    df = df.sort_values(["ticker", "ts_utc"]).reset_index(drop=True)
    return df

input.new_string:
def _normalize(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Canonical schema for the trades parquet.

    Actual raw Kalshi fields (all returned as strings):
      trade_id, ticker, count_fp, yes_price_dollars, no_price_dollars,
      taker_side, created_time

    Prices are already in dollars (e.g. "0.9900"), not cents — no /100.
    `count_fp` is the trade size; `_fp` = floating-point, since Kalshi
    supports fractional contract sizes when enabled on a market.
    """
    if "created_time" in df.columns:
        df["ts_utc"] = pd.to_datetime(df["created_time"], format="ISO8601", utc=True, errors="coerce")

    # Cast string -> numeric for the numeric fields
    for col in ("yes_price_dollars", "no_price_dollars", "count_fp"):
        if col in df.columns:
            df[col] = pd.to_numeric(df[col], errors="coerce")

    for col in ("ticker", "trade_id", "taker_side"):
        if col in df.columns:
            df[col] = df[col].astype("string")

    # Derived: signed size + mid-ish price. `taker_side` is 'yes'/'no';
    # side_sign = +1 when taker bought YES, -1 when taker bought NO.
    if "taker_side" in df.columns and "count_fp" in df.columns:
        df["signed_size"] = df["count_fp"] * df["taker_side"].map({"yes": 1.0, "no": -1.0}).astype(float)

    preferred = [
        "ts_utc", "ticker", "trade_id",
        "yes_price_dollars", "no_price_dollars",
        "count_fp", "taker_side", "signed_size",
        "created_time",
    ]
    order = [c for c in preferred if c in df.columns] + [c for c in df.columns if c not in preferred]
    df = df[order]
    df = df.sort_values(["ticker", "ts_utc"]).reset_index(drop=True)
    return df

content.text:
Full fetch done. Output:

## `data/kalshi/nba_trades_50games.parquet` — 20 MB

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Total trades | **590,968** |
| Unique tickers (games) | **50** (all 50 completed) |
| Time range | 2025-04-24 → 2026-02-21 |
| Median trades/game | **9,221** |
| Max trades/game | 39,496 (DEN-MIL, Jan 23) |
| Min trades/game | 521 (CLE-MIA, Apr 26) |
| Wall time | ~2-3 min |
| Endpoint | `/markets/trades` (public, no auth required) |

## Schema (per trade)

```
ts_utc              datetime64[ns, UTC]    trade timestamp, sub-second precision
ticker              string                 e.g. "KXNBAGAME-26FEB20INDWAS-WAS"
trade_id            string                 unique UUID
yes_price_dollars   float64                price paid for YES contract (0.00-1.00)
no_price_dollars    float64                = 1 - yes_price (sanity-checked: always sums to 1.0)
count_fp            float64                trade size in contracts (supports fractional)
taker_side          string                 "yes" or "no" — who aggressed
signed_size         float64                = count_fp * (+1 if taker=yes, -1 if taker=no)
created_time        string                 raw ISO8601 for traceability
```

## What you have now, two corresponding datasets

```
data/kalshi/
├── nba_1m_candles.parquet       360 KB   15,979 minutes of OHLC+bid/ask for 50 games
└── nba_trades_50games.parquet   20 MB    590,968 individual trade prints
```

These join on `ticker` — and you can bucket trades into any granularity you want (per-second, per-10-second) and compare against the 1-min candle closes.

## Three things the trades data unlocks that candles didn't

1. **Signed order flow** (`signed_size`) — the difference between Polymarket's trades (unsigned) and Kalshi's (signed with `taker_side`). Classic microstructure feature; angle C-adjacent work is now materially easier.
2. **Sub-minute resolution** — Kalshi's oldest timestamps are fractional-second. You can build 1-second / 5-second / 10-second bars from this, matching or beating Polymarket's trade-level granularity.
3. **Exact arbitrage check** — `yes_price + no_price = 1.00` is enforced at the trade level (verified). So any deviation of Polymarket's implied-YES from Kalshi's YES at the same moment is a real cross-venue mispricing candidate.

## Small housekeeping

- Both files are under `data/kalshi/` which is gitignored (under `/data/`) — they don't accidentally end up in git.
- Fetcher script at `scripts/fetch_kalshi_trades.py` (committable).
- Signing code is in place (dormant) — when you get a Kalshi API key for higher-rate work (e.g., pulling 1,000 games), just set `KALSHI_API_KEY_ID` + `KALSHI_PRIVATE_KEY_PATH` env vars and it'll flip to authenticated mode automatically.

## Next natural step

Want to scale up the universe now (pull candles + trades for all 970 games that Kalshi has NBA metadata for — or a subset)? That would run overnight-feasible and give you the larger sample for statistical claims. Or would you rather build the loader/event-stream side first (Phase 2 from earlier — turn this parquet pair into `MinuteBar` events for the backtest runner)?

input.content:
"""Build the Kalshi NBA game universe from the metadata CSV.

Source: the "Kalshi All NBA Contracts" sheet (exported as CSV).
        1,940 rows = 970 games x 2 team-contracts. Each row is one ticker.

Output: data/kalshi/nba_markets.parquet
        One row per game (970 rows), one ticker per game (the deterministic
        first-team-by-abbrev side — both sides would double our fetch volume
        for redundant YES/NO arbitrage-linked data).

Kept columns (minimum required for downstream fetchers + paper joins):
  ticker, event_ticker, yes_sub_title, no_sub_title,
  open_time, close_time, result, settlement_value_dollars, title.

Usage:
  python -m scripts.load_kalshi_universe
  python -m scripts.load_kalshi_universe --input "data/NBA_Data - Kalshi All NBA Contracts.csv"
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import logging
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd

log = logging.getLogger(__name__)


KEEP_COLUMNS = [
    "ticker", "event_ticker", "yes_sub_title", "no_sub_title",
    "open_time", "close_time", "result", "settlement_value_dollars", "title",
]


def load_universe(input_path: Path) -> pd.DataFrame:
    df = pd.read_csv(input_path, low_memory=False)

    missing = [c for c in KEEP_COLUMNS if c not in df.columns]
    if missing:
        raise KeyError(f"expected columns missing from source: {missing}")

    df = df[KEEP_COLUMNS].copy()

    # Dtypes
    df["open_time"]  = pd.to_datetime(df["open_time"],  format="ISO8601", utc=True, errors="coerce")
    df["close_time"] = pd.to_datetime(df["close_time"], format="ISO8601", utc=True, errors="coerce")
    df["settlement_value_dollars"] = pd.to_numeric(df["settlement_value_dollars"], errors="coerce")
    for c in ("ticker", "event_ticker", "yes_sub_title", "no_sub_title", "result", "title"):
        df[c] = df[c].astype("string")

    # Pick one ticker per event_ticker — sort by ticker and take the first.
    # Deterministic, stable across re-runs; swap sides later via --both-sides if needed.
    df = df.sort_values("ticker").drop_duplicates("event_ticker", keep="first").reset_index(drop=True)
    return df


def main() -> int:
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description=__doc__)
    parser.add_argument("--input", type=Path,
                        default=Path("data/NBA_Data - Kalshi All NBA Contracts.csv"))
    parser.add_argument("--output", type=Path, default=Path("data/kalshi/nba_markets.parquet"))
    parser.add_argument("--log-level", default="INFO")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    logging.basicConfig(level=args.log_level, format="%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(name)s: %(message)s")

    df = load_universe(args.input)

    args.output.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    df.to_parquet(args.output, index=False, compression="zstd")
    log.info("wrote %s (%d games, %.1f KB)", args.output, len(df), args.output.stat().st_size / 1024)

    print(f"\n--- {args.output} ---")
    print(f"games:               {len(df):,}")
    print(f"open_time range:     {df.open_time.min()}  ->  {df.open_time.max()}")
    print(f"close_time range:    {df.close_time.min()}  ->  {df.close_time.max()}")
    settled = ((df.settlement_value_dollars == 0) | (df.settlement_value_dollars == 1)).sum()
    print(f"clean settlement:    {settled}/{len(df)}")
    return 0


if __name__ == "__main__":
    raise SystemExit(main())

    settled = ((df.settlement_value_dollars == 0) | (df.settlement_value_dollars == 1)).sum()
    print(f"clean settlement:    {settled}/{len(df)}")
    return 0


if __name__ == "__main__":
    raise SystemExit(main())
